core_behavior: The system must detect and handle invalid users in a way that prevents errors, exceptions, or security vulnerabilities.
When an invalid user is encountered, the system will take alternative actions to ensure continued functionality and security.
inputs: User identification data (e.g., username, ID, email)
User authentication data (e.g., password, token, biometric data)
System configuration settings (e.g., authentication protocols, user roles)
outputs: Error messages or notifications indicating that the user is invalid
Redirects to login or registration pages
System logs recording the attempt with the invalid user
assumptions: An 'invalid user' is defined as a user who does not exist in the system's user database, has an incorrect password, or lacks the necessary permissions to access a particular resource.
The system uses a database to store user information and authentication data.
The system has a defined set of user roles with associated permissions.
The system will use a combination of client-side and server-side validation to check user input.
failure_modes: If the system's user database is unavailable, the system will return a generic error message indicating that it cannot verify user credentials at this time.
If the system encounters an unexpected error while processing user authentication, it will log the error and display a generic error message to the user.
If the system detects a potential security threat (e.g., multiple failed login attempts), it will trigger additional security measures (e.g., account lockout, CAPTCHA challenge).
edge_cases: A user with a similar name to an existing user attempts to register — the system will prevent registration and prompt the user to choose a different username.
A user attempts to access a resource without being logged in — the system will redirect the user to the login page.

A user's account is deleted or deactivated while they are still logged in — the system will log the user out and redirect them to the login page with a notification indicating that their account is no longer active.
security_constraints: The system must protect against common web attacks (e.g., SQL injection, cross-site scripting) when handling user input.
The system must use secure protocols for authentication and data transmission (e.g., HTTPS, TLS).
The system must limit the number of failed login attempts to prevent brute-force attacks.
The system must store user passwords securely using a salted hash.
performance_constraints: The system must respond to user authentication requests within a reasonable time frame (e.g., 2 seconds).
The system must be able to handle a high volume of concurrent user authentication requests without significant performance degradation.
The system must use efficient algorithms and data structures to minimize the computational overhead of user authentication and validation.
